The comparative effects of probiotics on growth, antioxidant indices and intestinal histomorphology of broilers under heat stress condition
===========================================================================================================================================

*   [Seyed Hamidreza Hashemitabar](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-024-66301-9#auth-Seyed_Hamidreza-Hashemitabar-Aff1)[1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-024-66301-9#Aff1)&
*   [Seyedeh Alemeh Hosseinian](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-024-66301-9#auth-Seyedeh_Alemeh-Hosseinian-Aff1)[1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-024-66301-9#Aff1)

[_Scientific Reports_](https://www.nature.com/srep)**volume 14**, Article number:23471 (2024) [Cite this article](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-024-66301-9#citeas)

*   4031 Accesses

*   4 Citations

*   1 Altmetric

*   [Metrics details](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-024-66301-9/metrics)

Abstract
--------

Heat stress adversely affects both the productivity and well-being of chickens. Probiotics offer beneficial impacts on the health and growth performance of broilers. The current study investigates the influence of administering of _Bacillus_ (including _B. subtilis_, _B. licheniformis_, _B. coagulans_, and _B. indicus_) and _Lactobacillus_ (consisting of _L. acidophilus_, _L. plantarum_, _L. buchneri_, and _L. rhamnosus_) probiotics via drinking water, either singular or combined, on various aspects including growth performance, oxidative stress markers, carcass characteristics, fecal microbial composition, intestinal structure, and intestinal pH in broilers exposed to chronic heat stress. A total of 150 one-day-old broiler chicks were divided into 5 groups: (1) NC, negative control; (2) HS, birds exposed to chronic heat stress; (3) HSpBacil, exposed to chronic heat stress and received _Bacillus_ probiotic; (4) HSpLAB, subjected to chronic heat stress and provided with _Lactobacillus_ probiotic; (5) HSpMix, subjected to chronic heat stress and administered a combined probiotic from _Bacillus_ and _Lactobacillus_. The HS group exhibited significantly reduced levels of growth performance, carcass traits, and notably affected oxidative stress indices, as well as intestinal pH and histomorphology in the birds. Additionally, the administered probiotics led to increased weight of lymphoid organs, enhanced body weight gain, and improved intestinal histomorphology. Furthermore, the probiotics decreased malondialdehyde and increased total antioxidant capacity in broilers. In conclusion, _Bacillus_ and _Lactobacillus_ probiotics, as single or multi-species, particularly _Lactobacillus_ and combined probiotic, demonstrated potential in alleviating the adverse effects of heat stress in broiler chickens. They could serve as beneficial feed additives and growth enhancers.

Introduction

In experimental studies, probiotics can be administered to the feed or drinking water of broiler chickens; and the diet is most frequently used[19](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-024-66301-9#ref-CR19 "Jiang, T. et al. While probiotics are typically added to feed in experimental settings, administering them through drinking water is advantageous, particularly to ensure viability in heat-sensitive strains and achieve uniform distribution probiotic[21](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-024-66301-9#ref-CR21 "Liu, X., Champagne, C. Hence, the hypothesis posits that administering probiotics can improve the health and productivity of broiler chickens subjected to chronic heat stress. However, there is limited research comparing the effects of administering probiotic mixtures containing of _Bacillus_ and _Lactobacillus_ either individually or in combination, via drinking water in broilers under chronic heat stress. Therefore, this study aimed to investigate the effects of multi-strain _Bacillus_ and _Lactobacillus_ probiotics, administered singly or in combination, on growth performance, carcass traits, oxidative stress indices, intestinal morphology, fecal microflora, and pH of intestinal contents in broiler chickens subjected to chronic heat stress. In the current study, the overarching objective was to determine the most effective probiotic combination for improving the health condition of broiler chicks under heat stress conditions.

Materials and methods
---------------------

### Ethics declaration

The current experiment adhered to animal welfare guidelines and experimental protocols established by the Research Ethics Committee of Shiraz University. Approval was granted by the Laboratory Animal Management Committee of Shiraz University (Approval No. 9730100/400). During the experiment, every effort was made to minimize bird suffering. All procedures were conducted in c accordance with the standards issued by ARRIVE (Animal Research: Reporting of In Vivo Experiments) guidelines.

### Broiler chickens and experimental design

The present study was performed at the Avian Diseases Research Center within the School of Veterinary Medicine. A total of 150 one-day-old broiler chicks (Cobb-500) were provided from a commercial hatchery poultry corporation (Shiraz, Fars Province, Iran), and then the chicks were randomly allocated into five groups, each comprising three replicates with 10 birds per replicate. Each group was housed in a separate temperature-controlled room, and each replicate was placed in a distinct wire cage (150 cm length × 100 cm width × 80 cm height). This experiment persisted for 42 days with a lightening program lasting 23 h per day throughout the study. The treatment groups were designated as follows: (1) negative control (NC): Chicks kept at the thermoneutral zone without probiotic administration; (2) heat-stressed group (HS): Chicks subjected to chronic heat stress without probiotic administration; (3) HSpBacil: Chicks subjected to chronic heat stress and received probiotic type 1, consisting of four strains of _Bacillus_ probiotic (listed the following); (4) HSpLAB: Chicks subjected to chronic heat stress and received probiotic type 2, comprising four strains of _Lactobacillus_ probiotic (listed the following); (5) HSpMix: Broilers subjected to chronic heat stress and received probiotic type 3, a combination of probiotic types 1 and 2. Throughout this study, the basal diets were based mainly on maize and soybean meal at three distinct feeding phases, including the starter diet (1 to 10 days of age), the grower diet (11 to 24 days of age), and the finisher diet (25 to 42 days of age) (Table [1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-024-66301-9#Tab1)). These diets were formulated to meet the nutrient requirements recommended by the National Research Council[24](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-024-66301-9#ref-CR24 "NRC. Nutrient Requirements of Poultry (National Academy Press, 1994)."). Throughout the experimental period, Birds had _ad libitum_ access to food and water.

**Table 1 Ingredients and nutrition composition of the basal diets at various feeding phase (%).**

[Full size table](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-024-66301-9/tables/1)

### Chronic heat stress induction

In this experiment, broiler chickens were initially raised at a temperature of 32–34℃ for the first three days. Subsequently, the temperature was gradually reduced to a constant temperature of around 24 ± 1℃, maintained until the birds reached 21 days of age. From day 21 to day 42, the NC group was reared at a normal temperature (24 ± 1℃ for 24 h per day), while the HS, HSpBacil, HSpLAB, and HSpMix groups were exposed to cyclic chronic heat stress. The heat-stressed groups were subjected to cyclic heat stress at 38 ± 1℃ for 8 h (from 9:00 a.m. to 5:00 p.m.) followed by rearing at an ambient temperature of 24 ± 1℃ for 16 h (from 5:00 p.m. to 9:00 a.m.)[25](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-024-66301-9#ref-CR25 "Mirsaiidi Farahani, M. & Hosseinian, S. A. Effects of dietary stinging nettle (Urtica dioica) on hormone stress and selected serum biochemical parameters of broilers subjected to chronic heat stress. Vet. Med. Sci. 8, 660–667. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1002/vms3.721
                  
                 (2022).").

### Compound probiotic preparation

In this study, from 21 days until 42 days of age, the HSpBacil, HSpLAB, and HSpMix groups received three different probiotic cocktails via drinking water. The HSpBacil group received probiotic type 1, containing four strains of _Bacillus_ (namely _B. subtilis_, _B. licheniformis_, _B. coagulans_, and _B. indicus_) in a 1:1:1:1 ratio, with a concentration of 4 × 10 9 colony-forming unit (CFU) per gram. Similarly, the HSpLAB group received probiotic type 2, containing four probiotic strains of _Lactobacillus_ (consisting of _L. acidophilus_, _L. plantarum_, _L. buchneri_, and _L. rhamnosus_) in a 1:1:1:1 ratio, also at a concentration of 4 × 10 9 CFU/g. The HSpMix group received a combined probiotic cocktail (probiotic type 3), comprising eight various strains of _Bacillus_ and _Lactobacillus_, as mentioned earlier, also at a concentration of 4 × 10 9 CFU/g. These probiotic bacteria were obtained from Pardis Roshd Mehregan Company (Shiraz, Fars Province, Iran) and were in lyophilized forms. During the entire experimental period, the probiotic powders were suspended daily in drinking water (at a rate of 1 gr per liter) to obtain the compound probiotics at a total daily dosage of 4 × 10 9 CFU/bird in the HSpBacil, HSpLAB, and HSpMix groups. During the experiment, the NC and HS groups received fresh drinking water without probiotics.

### Growth performance assay

During the experimental trial, all broilers were individually weighed every week following a 12 h feed deprivation. The body weight and daily feed consumption are recorded per replicate to calculate the average body weight (BW), average daily body weight gain (BWG), average daily feed intake (DFI), and average feed conversion ratio (FCR). These terms were recorded at three different phases, including starter (0–21 days), grower (22–42 days), and overall (1–42 days) phases, on a replicate basis. Additionally, health status and mortality were monitored and recorded daily throughout the entire experiment.

### Carcass characteristics assay

On days 21, 32, and 42 of the experiment, four broilers from each treatment group (one bird per replicate) were randomly chosen, weighed, and humanely euthanized via cervical dislocation to determine carcass traits. Following euthanasia, the birds were de-feathered, and various organs including breast muscle, bursa of Fabricius, spleen, liver, and intestine were carefully removed and weighed using a digital scale (± 0.01). In addition, the intestinal length was measured and documented for each bird.

### Intestinal histomorphological and goblet cell number assay

On days 21, 32, and 42 of the experiment, the ileum and jejunum from four birds per treatment group (one bird per replicate) were used for histological analysis. Sections of approximately 2 cm from of the jejunum (defined as the gut length from the end of the duodenal loop to the origin of Meckel's diverticulum) and ileum (from Meckel’s diverticulum to the ileocecal junction) were collected. ABSTRACT
--------

Remarkable changes have occurred in [poultry farming](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/poultry-farming "Learn more about poultry farming from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and meat processing in recent years, driven by advancements in breeding technology, feed processing technology, farming conditions, and management practices. The incorporation of [probiotics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/probiotic "Learn more about probiotics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [prebiotics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/food-science/prebiotics "Learn more about prebiotics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and phytoextracts has made significant contributions to the development of poultry meat products that promote both health and functionality throughout the growth phase and during meat processing.

Poultry fed with these substances improve meat quality, while incorporating probiotics, [prebiotics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/prebiotics "Learn more about prebiotics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and phytoextracts in poultry processing, as additives or supplements, inhibits pathogens and offers health benefits to consumers. However, it is vital to assess the safety of functional [fermented meat products](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/fermented-meat-products "Learn more about fermented meat products from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") containing these compounds and their potential effects on consumer health. Currently, there's still uncertainty in these aspects. Additionally, research on utilizing next-generation probiotic strains and synergistic combinations of probiotics and prebiotics in poultry meat products is in its early stages. Therefore, further investigation is required to gain a comprehensive understanding of the beneficial effects and safety considerations of these substances in poultry meat products in the future. This review offered a comprehensive overview of the applications of probiotics and prebiotics in poultry farming, focusing on their effects on nutrient utilization, growth efficiency, and gut health. Furthermore, potential of probiotics, prebiotics, and phytoextracts in enhancing poultry meat production was explored for improved health benefits and functionality, and possible issues associated with the use of these substances were discussed. Moreover, the conclusions drawn from this review and potential future perspectives in this field are presented.

INTRODUCTION
------------

Due to the evolving perceptions of meat consumption and their increasing awareness of [nutrition](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/food-science/nutrition "Learn more about nutrition from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and health, the meat consumption market was currently undergoing a significant transformation ([Kleyn and Ciacciariello, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0032579123008064#bib0090)). In 2023, global poultry meat production surpassed pork production by 17.61 million metric tons, reaching a total of 139.68 million metric tons ([Figure 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0032579123008064#fig0001)). The poultry meat consumption market is experiencing rapid growth, projected to increase by 65% from 2015 to 2035, surpassing the growth rates of eggs (50%) and pork (35%) ([Figure 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0032579123008064#fig0002)). Poultry meat, such as chicken, duck, and goose, fulfills the nutritional requirements of a contemporary healthy diet, characterized by being high in protein, low in calories, low in fat and low in cholesterol, and having a high digestive absorption rate ([Wołoszyn et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0032579123008064#bib0178); [Goluch et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0032579123008064#bib0064)). However, the high protein and [moisture content](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/moisture "Learn more about moisture content from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of poultry meat made it susceptible to spoilage and deterioration. This susceptibility was particularly evident during processing, storage, transport, and sales due to the high vulnerability of [unsaturated fatty acids](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/unsaturated-fatty-acid "Learn more about unsaturated fatty acids from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") to [oxidation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/alpha-oxidation "Learn more about oxidation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and rotting ([Katiyo et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0032579123008064#bib0085)). Consequently, the quality and [nutritional value](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/food-science/nutritive-value "Learn more about nutritional value from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of the products decline while toxic and harmful substances may even be formed ([Tsafrakidou et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0032579123008064#bib0173)).

[Probiotics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/probiotic "Learn more about Probiotics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") can be used alone or in combination with other additives, generally regarded as safe ([Biswas et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0032579123008064#bib0027)). [Prebiotics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/food-science/prebiotics "Learn more about Prebiotics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") are health-promoting substrates for microbes, encompassing fiber, polyphenols, [polyunsaturated fatty acids](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/food-science/polyunsaturated-fatty-acid "Learn more about polyunsaturated fatty acids from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and [conjugated linoleic acid](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/conjugated-linoleic-acid "Learn more about conjugated linoleic acid from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Peng et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0032579123008064#bib0136); [Biswas et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0032579123008064#bib0027); [Zhang et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0032579123008064#bib0187)). The incorporation of dietary probiotics, prebiotics and other beneficial substances can enhance the intestinal microbiota composition, augment the slaughter yield and ultimately elevate the quality of [livestock](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/farm-animal "Learn more about livestock from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and poultry meat ([Vlaicu et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0032579123008064#bib0175); [Wang et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0032579123008064#bib191)). When administered at effective dosages, these compounds have the potential to optimize the microbial equilibrium within the [gastrointestinal system](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/gastrointestinal-system "Learn more about gastrointestinal system from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of poultry hosts, thereby enhancing digestion and promoting overall well-being, as well as protection against detrimental bacteria and pathogens ([Jha et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0032579123008064#bib0081)). Moreover, a variety of approaches have been employed to reduce the fat content in poultry meat. These include the incorporation of microorganisms with probiotic functions ([Bis-Souza et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0032579123008064#bib0026)), the utilization of plant extracts and compounds ([Pérez-Burillo et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0032579123008064#bib0137)). These strategies not only contribute to minimizing the use of additives and ingredients associated with health disorders but also enhance the overall nutritional value of [poultry products](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/poultry-product "Learn more about poultry products from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages").

Probiotics, prebiotics, and phytoextracts significantly reduce antibiotic usage, enhance poultry meat quality, improve fat profile, prevent spoilage, extend the shelf life of fermented [poultry products](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/food-science/poultry-products "Learn more about poultry products from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and add health benefits to poultry meat ([Chandra et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0032579123008064#bib0034)). Produced by probiotics, prebiotics, and phytoextracts, poultry and its meat products meet public demands for antibiotic-free meats and functional foods with extra benefits ([Chowdhury et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0032579123008064#bib0039)). Aligned with the pursuit of a healthy lifestyle, these modifications can generally enhance public health by reducing the prevalence of chronic diseases like diabetes, obesity, fatty liver disease, and cardiovascular disease, as their lower fat content compared to conventionally produced poultry meat ([Das et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0032579123008064#bib0042)). The significance of these researches lies in their potential to offer valuable insights for both [poultry production](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/poultry-production "Learn more about poultry production from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and processing, thereby facilitating groundbreaking advancements in the field of [poultry science](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/poultry-science "Learn more about poultry science from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages").

This comprehensive review explores the diverse applications of probiotics, prebiotics, and phytoextracts in [poultry farming](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/poultry-farming "Learn more about poultry farming from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), highlighting their pivotal role in enhancing nutrient absorption, improving growth efficiency, and promoting digestive health. Additionally, it illuminates the vast potential of these agents for producing wholesome and functional poultry meat products, underscoring their significance within the food industry. In addition, the challenges associated with implementing these substances in poultry farming, offering valuable insights into potential obstacles and strategies for overcoming them. It concludes by emphasizing the necessity for more comprehensive research and exploration of the full potential of these substances in poultry farming, with the ultimate objective of producing healthier and more sustainable poultry meat products for global markets.

FACTORS INFLUENCING THE QUALITY OF POULTRY MEAT
-----------------------------------------------

The quality of poultry meat and its related products can be influenced by various factors, which can be broadly categorized into 2 main processes: poultry rearing and meat processing ([Figure 3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0032579123008064#fig0003)). During the process of poultry rearing, the quality of poultry meat is significantly impacted by genetic, nutritional, and environmental factors ([Baéza et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0032579123008064#bib0019)).

### Factors Influencing the Quality of Poultry Meat During Rearing

The nutritional composition of the diet plays a crucial role in determining the quality of poultry meat. A well-balanced ratio of essential nutrients in the diet leads to enhanced nutrient utilization and superior overall quality ([Jha et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0032579123008064#bib0081)). It is noteworthy that a negative correlation exists between the protein content in the feed and the proportion of fat in the live animal. This can be attributed to the increased energy demand for protein breakdown and reorganization, leading to reduced fat deposition ([Liebert, 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0032579123008064#bib0102); [Novodworski et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0032579123008064#bib0131)). Furthermore, the incorporation of additives into the diet has garnered recognition among numerous animal nutritionists for its profound impact on meat quality ([Alagawany et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0032579123008064#bib0007)). The inclusion of [vitamin E](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/vitamin-e "Learn more about vitamin E from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") has been demonstrated to enhance the oxidative stability of poultry meat ([Amevor et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0032579123008064#bib0013)) and found to increase the expression of matrix [metalloproteinases](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/metalloproteinase "Learn more about metalloproteinases from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (**MMPs**) within the muscle, increasing the proportion of soluble fiber in the muscle and promoting [lipogenesis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/lipogenesis "Learn more about lipogenesis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Archile-Contreras et al., 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0032579123008064#bib0015)). Additionally, [vitamin D3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/cholecalciferol "Learn more about vitamin D3 from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") improves the water retention capacity of poultry muscles ([Nong et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0032579123008064#bib0130)). Moreover, dietary calcium can reduce plasma cholesterol levels ([Matuszewski et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0032579123008064#bib0115)), while yeast selenium supplementation can increase the [micronutrient](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/food-science/micronutrient "Learn more about micronutrient from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") concentration in poultry meat ([Markovic et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0032579123008064#bib0112)).

In addition to these factors, specific substances have been found to exert an influence on the quality of poultry meat as well. For instance, betaine serves as a source of active methyl groups for metabolic processes within the organism, thereby augmenting the levels of flavor precursors such as creatine, creatinine, inosinic acid, and [myoglobin](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/myoglobin "Learn more about myoglobin from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in poultry meat ([Chen et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0032579123008064#bib0037)). The incorporation of chito-oligosaccharides in poultry diets has been demonstrated to ameliorate intestinal barrier damage, oxidative and immunological stress in lipopolysaccharide-challenged [laying hens](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/laying-hens "Learn more about laying hens from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), as well as enhance the [laying performance](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/laying-performance "Learn more about laying performance from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), egg quality, blood biochemistry, [antioxidant capacity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/food-science/antioxidant-capacity "Learn more about antioxidant capacity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and immune response in late-laying hens ([Xu et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0032579123008064#bib0183); [Gu et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0032579123008064#bib0066)). The incorporation of [citrus peel](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/citrus-peels "Learn more about citrus peel from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") powder has been demonstrated to enhance the freshness, sweetness, and aroma of broth while effectively eliminating any undesirable flavors ([Vlaicu et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0032579123008064#bib0175)). Additionally, polyphenols have been found to improve [meat tenderness](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/meat-tenderness "Learn more about meat tenderness from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), juiciness, and flavor while reducing cholesterol content, thereby contributing to the production of high-quality chicken meat ([Abdel Moneim et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0032579123008064#bib0003)).

### Factors Influencing the Quality of Poultry Meat During Meat Processing

During the processing of poultry, various factors can impact the quality of [poultry products](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/poultry-product "Learn more about poultry products from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). These factors encompass the techniques employed for slaughtering, transportation, storage, overall processing. The environmental conditions during storage play a critical role in determining the quality of poultry meat. Specifically, the [storage temperature](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/storage-temperature "Learn more about storage temperature from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") holds varying degrees of significance on the quality of poultry meat ([Zelenakova et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0032579123008064#bib0186)). Generally, meat products demonstrate superior quality when stored at lower temperatures. Therefore, it is imperative to meticulously regulate the storage conditions and duration of poultry meat in order to ensure its optimal quality and flavor ([Chen et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0032579123008064#bib0038)). Moreover, various techniques can be employed during poultry processing to sterilize the poultry and prolong its shelf life. These techniques encompass the utilization of preservatives, [probiotics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/probiotic "Learn more about probiotics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [prebiotics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/food-science/prebiotics "Learn more about prebiotics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), phytoextracts, radiation, high-pressure treatment, and thermal treatment ([Baéza et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0032579123008064#bib0019); [Lee et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0032579123008064#bib0099)).

## Abstract

### Background

The increase in global population has elevated the food demand which in turn escalated the food animal production systems, especially poultry industries. For a long time, antibiotics are used worldwide to safeguard animals from diseases and for high production performances. Over usage of antibiotics has led to severe side effects such as antibiotic resistance among pathogenic bacteria, harming the beneficial bacteria in the gut, and stacking up of residuals in animal food products. It is the need of the hour to find a competent alternative to antibiotics. Probiotics have gained major attention as safe, feasible, and efficient alternatives to commercial antibiotics.

### Main body

Probiotics meaning “*prolife*” are live, non-pathogenic microorganisms that when given in sufficient amount confer an advantage to the host health and well-being. Probiotics are reported to improve growth, production performance, immunity, and digestibility, safeguard gut microflora, and enhance egg and meat quality traits in poultry. Proper selection of probiotics strains is crucial before their commercialization. This systematic review focuses on the mechanism of action of probiotics and summarizes the potential role of different probiotics supplementation for enhancing the production and shielding the health and immunity of poultry flocks.

### Conclusions

Probiotics has got a beneficial impact on the health and immunity of poultry, showing their competence as an alternative to commercial antibiotics. Modern experimental techniques are required to shed more light on the capabilities of probiotics and their usage for animal health.

## Main text

### Mechanism of action of probiotics

Probiotics place a key role in gut microbial health. The mechanisms of action of probiotics mainly include two, i.e. competitive exclusion and immune system modulation. Competitive exclusion of pathogens by probiotics includes: (a) production of inhibitory compounds like bacteriocins, mucins, defensins, etc. (b) preventing the adhesion of pathogens, (c) competition for nutrients, (d) reduction of toxin bioavailability, and (e) modulation of the host immune system including the enhancement of both innate and adaptive immunity (Hernandez-Patlan et al., [2020](/articles/10.1186/s41936-024-00379-5#ref-CR76 "Hernandez-Patlan, D., Solis-Cruz, B., Hargis, B. M., & Tellez, G. (2020). The use of probiotics in poultry production for the control of bacterial infections and aflatoxins. Prebiotics Probiotics, 4, 217–238.")).

### a) Secretion of inhibitory compounds

Probiotics produce different types of inhibitory compounds that help in reducing pathogen invasion. These include “antimicrobial peptides (AMPs)”such as bacteriocins, hydrogen peroxide, organic acids, ethanol, and diacetyl (Liao & Nyachoti, [2017](/articles/10.1186/s41936-024-00379-5#ref-CR108 "Liao, S. F., & Nyachoti, C. M. (2017). Using probiotics to improve swine gut health and nutrient utilization. Animal Nutrition, 3, 331–343. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aninu.2017.06.007.
                  
                ")). Bacteriocins are ribosomal synthesized AMP that can eliminate or inhibit pathogenic bacterial strains. They are segregated depending on their size, structure, post-translational modifications (Cotter et al., [2013](/articles/10.1186/s41936-024-00379-5#ref-CR40 "Cotter, P. D., Ross, R. P., & Hill, C. (2013). Bacteriocins— a viable alternative to antibiotics? Natures Reviews Microbiology, 11, 95–105.")).

It has been reported that pediocin A produced by *Pediococcuspentosaceus* and divercin of *Carnobacteriumdivergens* has improved the broiler performance in a field trial when challenged with *C. perfringens* (Grilli et al., [2009](/articles/10.1186/s41936-024-00379-5#ref-CR68 "Grilli, E., Messina, M. R., Catelli, E., Morlacchini, M., & Piva, A. (2009). Pediocin A improves growth performance of broilers challenged with Clostridium perfringens. Poultry Science, 88, 2152–2158."); Józefiak et al., [2012](/articles/10.1186/s41936-024-00379-5#ref-CR86 "Józefiak, D., Sip, A., Rutkowski, A., Rawski, M., Kaczmarek, S., Wolun-Cholewa, M., Engberg, R. M., & Højberg, O. (2012). Lyophilized CarnobacteriumdivergensAS7 bacteriocin preparation improves the performance of broiler chickens challenged with Clostridium perfringens. Poultry Science, 91, 1899–1907.")). An inhibitory compound—nisin produced by *L. lactis*—affects the cells and spores of *C. perfringens* in *in vitro* conditions (Udompijitkul et al., [2012](/articles/10.1186/s41936-024-00379-5#ref-CR174 "Udompijitkul, P., Paredes-Sabja, D., & Sarker, M. R. (2012). Inhibitory effects of nisin against Clostridium perfringens food poisoning and non-food-borne isolates. Journal of Food Science, 77(M51), M56. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1750-3841.2011.02475.x
                  
                ")).

Bacteriocin secreted by gram-positive bacteria such as lactic acid bacteria (LAB), kill the pathogens by disrupting their cell wall synthesis or by making pores in them (Belguesmia et al., [2010](/articles/10.1186/s41936-024-00379-5#ref-CR22 "Belguesmia, Y., Choiset, Y., Prévost, H., Dalgalarrondo, M., Chobert, J. M., & Drider, D. (2010). Partial purification and characterization of the mode of action of enterocin S37: A bacteriocin produced by Enterococcus faecalis S37 isolated from poultry feces. Journal of Environmental and Public Health, 2010(1), 986460.")). LAB-bacteriocin does not affect other bacterial population in the microbiota as it targets specific species in the GI tract (Hernandez-Patlan et al., [2020](/articles/10.1186/s41936-024-00379-5#ref-CR76 "Hernandez-Patlan, D., Solis-Cruz, B., Hargis, B. M., & Tellez, G. (2020). The use of probiotics in poultry production for the control of bacterial infections and aflatoxins. Prebiotics Probiotics, 4, 217–238.")).

Synergistic effects of LAB-bacteriocins together with a few biomolecules are reported such as enterocin AS-48 and ethambutol against *M. tuberculosis* (Aguilar-Pérez et al., [2018](/articles/10.1186/s41936-024-00379-5#ref-CR5 "Aguilar-Pérez, C., Gracia, B., Rodrigues, L., Vitoria, A., Cebrián, R., Deboosère, N., Song, O. R., Brodin, P., Maqueda, M., & Aínsa, J. A. (2018). The synergy between circular bacteriocin AS-48 and ethambutol against Mycobacterium tuberculosis. Antimicrobial Agents and Chemotherapy, 62(9), e00359-e418. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1128/AAC.00359-18
                  
                ")), beta-lactams with nisin against *S. enterica* serovar *Typhimurium* (Rishi et al., [2014](/articles/10.1186/s41936-024-00379-5#ref-CR144 "Rishi, P., Preet Singh, A., Garg, N., & Rishi, M. (2014). Evaluation of nisin-β-lactam antibiotics against clinical strains of Salmonella enterica serovar Typhi. Journal of Antibiotics, 67, 807–811."); Singh et al., [2014](/articles/10.1186/s41936-024-00379-5#ref-CR160 "Singh, A. P., Preet, S., & Rishi, P. (2014). Nisin / beta-lactam adjunct therapy against Salmonella enterica serovar Typhimurium: a mechanistic approach. Journal of Antimicrobial Chemotherapy, 69, 1877–1887. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1093/jac/dku049
                  
                ")), Garvicin KA farnesol against the gram-negative and gram-positive bacteria (Chi & Holo, [2018](/articles/10.1186/s41936-024-00379-5#ref-CR36 "Chi, H., & Holo, H. (2018). Synergistic antimicrobial activity between the broad spectrum bacteriocin garvicin KS and nisin, farnesol and polymyxin B against gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria. Current Microbiology, 75, 272–277.")), citric acid with nisin against *L. monocytogenes* and *S. aureus* (Zhao et al., [2017](/articles/10.1186/s41936-024-00379-5#ref-CR188 "Zhao, X., Zhen, Z., Wang, X., & Guo, N. (2017). Synergy of a combination of nisin and citric acid against Staphylococcus aureus and Listeria monocytogenes. Food Additives and Contaminants Part a, Chemistry, Analysis, Control, Exposure & Risk Assessment, 34, 2058–2068. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1080/19440049.2017.1366076
                  
                ")). A strain of *Brevibacillus borstelensis* active in GI tract has anti-*C. perfringens* activity and is linked with a thermostable bacteriocin-like inhibitory substance (BLIS) of 12 kDa (Sharma et al., [2014](/articles/10.1186/s41936-024-00379-5#ref-CR154 "Sharma, N., Gupta, A., & Gautam, N. (2014). Characterization of bacteriocin like inhibitory substance produced by a new strain Brevibacillus borstelensis AG1 Isolated from ‘Marcha.’ Brazilian Journal of Microbiology: [publication of the Brazilian Society for Microbiology], 45(3), 1007–1015. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1590/s1517-83822014000300033
                  
                ")).

Organic acids exhibited inhibitory activities against pathogenic bacteria. They reduce the intracellular pH and stop the movement of the internal protons which in turn deplete the cellular energy (Ricke, [2003](/articles/10.1186/s41936-024-00379-5#ref-CR142 "Ricke, S. C. (2003). Perspectives on the use of organic acids and short chain fatty acids as antimicrobials. Poultry Science, 82, 632–639.")). Organic acids directly target the cytoplasmic membrane, cell wall, and particular metabolic functions of harmful bacteria leading to its disruption and depletion (Nair et al., [2017](/articles/10.1186/s41936-024-00379-5#ref-CR124 "Nair, S. M., Amalaradjou, M. A., & Venkitanarayanan, K. (2017). Anti-virulence properties of probiotics in combating microbial pathogenesis. Advances in Applied Microbiology, 98, 1–29."); Zhitnitsky et al., [2017](/articles/10.1186/s41936-024-00379-5#ref-CR190 "Zhitnitsky, D., Rose, J., & Lewinson, O. (2017). The highly synergistic, broad-spectrum, the antibacterial activity of organic acids and transition metals. Scientific Reports, 7, 44554. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1038/srep44554
                  
                ")).

Lactic acid bacteria produce lactic acid which generates an unfavourable condition in the gut of pathogenic bacteria (Dittoe et al., [2018](/articles/10.1186/s41936-024-00379-5#ref-CR47 "Dittoe, D. K., Ricke, S. C., & Kiess, A. S. (2018). Organic acids and potential for modifying the avian gastrointestinal tract and reducing pathogens and disease. Frontiers of Veterinary Science, 5, 216.")). Reports showed that lactic acid bacteria in a concentration of 0.5% (v/v) could inhibit the growth of pathogens such as *Salmonella* spp., *L. Monocytogenes,* or *E. coli* (Wang et al., [2015](/articles/10.1186/s41936-024-00379-5#ref-CR180 "Wang, C

enriched_points.expanded_content: Probiotics offer a natural, effective alternative to antibiotics in poultry, enhancing growth performance, gut health, and immunity while reducing disease incidence and promoting sustainable practices.
Further research is crucial to explore optimal probiotic strains and combinations, dosing strategies, and their long-term impacts on poultry health, productivity, and meat quality.

Integrating probiotics into poultry nutrition aligns with sustainable agriculture, reducing antibiotic reliance, improving animal welfare, and meeting consumer demands for healthier, antibiotic-free meat products.
